commonDeficiencies: PSecAA acceptance overdue—FAA ACO or EASA PCM has not formally accepted the PSecAA (UC-22.60.1).
CSI inventory incomplete—one or more airframe-type CSIs missing from the inventory (UC-22.60.2).
ARINC 811 segregation drift—administrative LAN (AISD) traffic reaches avionics (ACD), or PIESD reaches AISD (UC-22.60.3).
LSAP signed by unauthorised signer—engineering certificate used in place of the production certificate (UC-22.60.4).
Cyber-AD overdue—fleet contains tails out of compliance with a cyber-AD past its compliance deadline (UC-22.60.5).
PMAT non-compliant—maintenance laptop posture below baseline (UC-22.60.6).
EFB posture stale—EFB tablet has missing MDM check-in for > 7 days (UC-22.60.7).
ACARS / CPDLC injection—unexpected ATC clearance via ACARS (UC-22.60.8).
GNSS spoof / jam not surfaced—RAIM warnings not centralised (UC-22.60.9).
ADS-B injection—ghost target appearing on the cockpit display (UC-22.60.10).
Engine remote-monitoring without PAM—engine OEM remote session bypasses PAM (UC-22.60.11).
IFE-to-AISD traversal—passenger IFE-host reaches airline AISD (UC-22.60.12).
Part-IS 72-hour clock missed—EASA Part-IS preliminary report submitted late (UC-22.60.13).
ISMS audit overdue—Part-IS ISMS audit cycle longer than 12 months (UC-22.60.14).
SBOM CVE exposure unaddressed—fleet exposed to high-CVSS CVE on airborne component for > 30 days (UC-22.60.15).
Recurrent cyber training expired—flight crew without current cyber-incident training (UC-22.60.16).
Aeronautical database tampered—NAV / FMS database load with invalid signature (UC-22.60.17).
do-326a.testingApproach: FAA Aircraft Certification Office (ACO) cyber engineers review the PSecAA, CSI, SRA, security-architecture, and security-verification deliverables during TC/STC projects. EASA project certification managers (PCMs) perform the equivalent review for EASA-issued TCs and STCs. EASA Part-IS National Competent Authority (NCA) inspectors audit the ISMS under Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/203, with the inspection cycle typically aligned to the existing safety-oversight audit. In-service continuing-airworthiness review is conducted by FAA Aircraft Maintenance Division and EASA Continuing Airworthiness Department, often through the IATA Operational Safety Audit (IOSA) framework. The Splunk catalogue's UC-22.60.x evidence chain produces auditable rows for each of these workflows. The Accountable Manager self-tests quarterly via a synthetic-incident exercise that exercises UC-22.60.13 and UC-22.60.14 without crossing into real authority notifications.
do-326a.reportingCadence: Cyber-incident notification: EASA Part-IS IS.OR.230 - within 72 hours of detection (preliminary report); within 1 month (final report). FAA: contemporaneous with the operational impact under 14 CFR 121. NCA-specific timelines under each Member-State NIS2 transposition law (where overlapping). TC-holder cyber-bulletin distribution: within the contractually-agreed window (typically 30-90 days for SB; immediate for AD). EASA Part-IS ISMS audit cycle: at least annual, typically aligned to the existing SMS audit cycle. Personnel cyber-incident training: at least annual.
do-326a.penaltyStructure: Enforcement at four layers: (1) Type-certification stage (FAA ACO / EASA PCM) - non-compliance can result in a finding of non-compliance that delays the type-certificate issuance; programme delays of 3-12 months are common; revenue impact runs to hundreds of millions of dollars per programme. (2) Continuing-airworthiness stage (FAA Aircraft Maintenance Division / EASA Continuing Airworthiness Department / NCA) - non-compliance with a cyber-AD can result in an emergency Airworthiness Directive grounding the affected fleet; cyber-ADs may include a no-despatch clause that takes effect immediately. (3) EASA Part-IS / NIS2 (NCA inspection) - Part-IS non-compliance can lead to corrective-action plans, suspension of approval, or where NIS2 overlap applies, administrative fines up to EUR 10 million or 2 % of worldwide annual turnover. (4) Air Operator Certificate (AOC) - failure to maintain the SMS-integrated ISMS under EASA Part-IS or the FAA-equivalent can lead to suspension or revocation of the AOC. Personal liability: the Accountable Manager signs the operator-certificate attestation; in some EU/EEA Member States this can rise to administrative or criminal liability for a Part-IS / NIS2 failure leading to a safety-impacting incident. In the US, 14 CFR Part 119 places the Chief Pilot, Director of Operations, and Director of Maintenance under personal accountability.
